coredata.dc:title: Thermal management strategies for lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles: A comprehensive review of nanofluid-based battery thermal management systems
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Gasmelseed, Abdelrahman
Ismael, Mhadi A.
Said, Mior A.
Ahmad, Faiz
Osman, Sohaib
coredata.dc:description: Electric vehicles have recently experienced rapid growth in battery heat generation rates due to increasing commercial demands for faster vehicle speeds and higher charging rates. This growth intensified the importance of innovative thermal management strategies to ensure safe and efficient vehicle operation. This paper aims to comprehensively review and discuss recent research investigating nanofluid battery thermal management systems (BTMS). Nanofluids are proposed as promising coolants as they possess enhanced thermal performance owing to their higher thermal conductivity compared to conventional fluids. Studies investigating nanofluid BTMSs can be divided into experimental and numerical studies investigating the effects of varying base fluid, nanoparticle type and concentration on thermal performance and pumping power. The overall trend of the reviewed studies displays an improvement in thermal performance with the increase of nanoparticles concentration, coupled with higher pumping power due to the higher viscosity values resulting in higher friction, with reviewed studies demonstrating nanofluids BTMS thermal improvements in the range of 2.9 – 30.5 % with pressure drop increase in the range of 14 – 70 % compared to the base fluid. The most commonly investigated types of nanoparticles are aluminium oxide (Al2O3), copper oxide (CuO), and silver oxide (AgO), with concentrations in the range of 0.1 – 5 %. The review highlighted the lack of long-term stability investigation and hybrid nanofluid BTMS studies in addition to studies evaluating the economic and environmental effects of utilising nanofluids in liquid BTMSs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of recent BTMS review papers.

list-item.para: Air BTMSs are energy efficient and economical but have low thermal efficacy, liquid BTMSs possess high energy efficiency at the cost of complexity and weight, PCM BTMSs are cost and energy effective, however, they increase the mass of the vehicle, HP BTMSs offer good thermal conductivity, but they suffer from low contact surface area, TE BTMSs are light and compact, but they are costly and energy intensive.

Recommendations for future work including the importance of additional research on PCM BTMSs specifically regarding addition of nanoparticles, the use of nanofluids in liquid BTMSs, and additional research on hybrid BTMSs.
Importance of separators for lithium-ion batteries considering safety and efficiency. The use of fibre separators improves electrolyte wetting and ionic conductivity.
The addition of thermally conductive additives to PCM BTMS must take into account the issue of particle agglomeration which can be circumvented by employing either porous material or dispersants.
Hybrid PCM BTMSs provide better thermal performance compared to basic PCM BTMSs

Recommendation for future work including investigation of PCM cycle life, additional research on bio-based PCM, and further research into the possibility of using artificial intelligence to optimise PCM BTMSs.
The use of hybrid HP BTMSs is demanded due to the failure to remove heat from the basic HP condenser.
The most common formation of HP is the placement of the HP between or below the cells.
The potential of utilising HP BTMS for industrial EV demands for fast charging.

Recommendation for future work including additional research on heating performance analysis of BTMSs, further investigation into utilisation of non-toxic, non-flammable, non-polluting, and lightweight material for BTMS, the review also recommends analysing BTMS performance not just in terms of temperature but also weight, cost, and energy consumption.

row.entry: All BTMSs with a focus on HP BTMSs

list-item.para: Air BTMSs are cheap and have simple designs with low thermal efficiency and efficacy, liquid BTMSs provide better heat transfer with better temperature uniformity at the expense of sophisticated design, increased weight, and higher cost. PCM BTMSs have long lifetimes with good temperature uniformity, however, they suffer from poor thermal conductivity and low commercial utilisation, HP BTMSs possess good thermal conductivity, longevity and efficiency, but they suffer from the problem of drying out.
HP and PCM BTMSs were found to be better compared to liquid and air BTMSs considering the same battery load. Furthermore, hybrid BTMSs were found to be better compared to basic BTMSs.
Recommendation for future work including additional attention to improve hybrid BTMSs in terms of weight, cost, and volume.

row.entry: All BTMSs with a focus on liquid BTMSs

list-item.para: Air BTMSs are simple, and require less maintenance, energy, and cost but they suffer from low thermal conductivity. Liquid BTMSs have good thermal efficiency and temperature uniformity, but they require complicated and heavy equipment, PCM BTMSs have reduced equipment and provide passive thermal management, but they suffer from low thermal conductivity and efficiency. HP BTMSs have good heat transfer potential but suffer from low surface contact area limiting their potential.
The most effective liquid BTMS optimisation methods are evaporative cooling, spray cooling, and nanofluids
Recommendation for future work regarding the incorporation of hybrid coolants considering toxicity, availability, and cost.

The experimental tests of the battery (XPS and SEM tests) correlate the battery failure to electrolyte degradation.

list-item.para: Temperature and voltage variation with time
Effect of ambient temperature variation on thermal runaway mechanisms
SOH and SOC charge variation effects on thermal runaway mechanisms
Thermal runaway evolution can be split into four distinct phases.
The second stage is characterised by noticeable side reaction occurrence.
The third stage is characterised by gas explosion and combustion.
The fourth stage is characterised by the occurrence of the thermal runaway.
Conduction and radiation cause the highest fraction of heat generation during thermal runaway.
The most severe scenario for thermal runaway occurrence is high ambient temperature, high SOC, and low SOH.

row.entry:
Experimental:
Thermal runaway test
Numerical:
Association rule mining multiparameter model

list-item.para: Initial temperature variation effect on pressure, temperature, and voltage
Battery variables response to different warning levels
Thermal runaway can be divided into four phases depending on the voltage value. It can also be divided into three phases based on the temperature value.
Battery pressure value can be used as an early sign parameter owing to its quick response time and cheap detection methods.
Thermal runaway can be prevented during the first three warning levels with varying degrees of damage and at the fourth stage the thermal runaway is unavoidable.
Temperature and temperature change rate variation at the top and bottom of the battery with time
Heat generation variation at the bottom and top of the battery with time
Thermal runaway is characterised by a gradual increase in temperature up to 190 °C, followed by a sharp increase to 509 °C.

Taking into account the effect of combustion in thermal runaway, a rapid temperature increase was observed at low temperatures (60 °C). This modified behaviour is attributed to variations in internal reaction models.

At low temperatures, the decomposition of solid electrolyte interphase significantly contributed to generated heat (90 % heat generated), at higher temperatures (170 to 200 °C) anode-electrolyte reactions played a major role in temperature increase. At extremely high temperatures (200 °C and above) the highest heat generated was due to cathode-electrolyte reactions.
Heat generation and temperature variation at different states of charge
Effect of initial temperature variation on thermal runaway
Maximum temperature during thermal runaway is directly proportional to the discharge rate.
Time to reach maximum temperature is inversely proportional to the discharge rate.
The maximum thermal runaway temperature is directly proportional to the SOC (caused by the higher potential difference between electrodes).

At the cell level within a module, cells experience simultaneous and consecutive propagation, whereas modules experience simultaneous propagation.
Gas evolution drastically increases the cell pressure reaching up to 6.3 kPa and therefore proper ventilation is crucial for safe operation to prevent explosion.

Thermal runaway was found to be divided into three different phases, namely exterior heating (13.31 °C/min temperature rise), rapid heating (21.17 °C/min temperature rise), and finally the thermal runaway incident.
Nonlinear heating caused thermal runaway to occur an average of 4.5 times earlier compared to the linear heating model.
Fully charged batteries (100 % SOC) experienced the most severe thermal runaway compared to other states of charge batteries.
The main elements detected in the erupted thermal runaway particulates are electrode materials including oxygen (O), carbon (C) and nickel (Ni).

Lower SOC batteries require higher heating power compared to higher SOC Batteries (9.5 W at 100 % SOC compared to 15 W at 30 %).
Three stages of thermal runaway were identified mainly, heating stage, venting stage, and finally thermal runaway occurrence.

Soot particles resulting from battery thermal runaway vary in size from 0.349 to 0.412 nm.
The elements resulting from thermal runaway in descending order of quantity are carbon (C), oxygen (O), Lithium (Li), fluorine (F), and phosphorus (P).

head.ce:title: Thermal management strategies for lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles: A comprehensive review of nanofluid-based battery thermal management systems

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electric vehicles have recently experienced rapid growth in battery heat generation rates due to increasing commercial demands for faster vehicle speeds and higher charging rates. This growth intensified the importance of innovative thermal management strategies to ensure safe and efficient vehicle operation. This paper aims to comprehensively review and discuss recent research investigating nanofluid battery thermal management systems (BTMS). Nanofluids are proposed as promising coolants as they possess enhanced thermal performance owing to their higher thermal conductivity compared to conventional fluids. Studies investigating nanofluid BTMSs can be divided into experimental and numerical studies investigating the effects of varying base fluid, nanoparticle type and concentration on thermal performance and pumping power. The overall trend of the reviewed studies displays an improvement in thermal performance with the increase of nanoparticles concentration, coupled with higher pumping power due to the higher viscosity values resulting in higher friction, with reviewed studies demonstrating nanofluids BTMS thermal improvements in the range of 2.9 – 30.5 % with pressure drop increase in the range of 14 – 70 % compared to the base fluid. The most commonly investigated types of nanoparticles are aluminium oxide (Al2O3), copper oxide (CuO), and silver oxide (AgO), with concentrations in the range of 0.1 – 5 %. The review highlighted the lack of long-term stability investigation and hybrid nanofluid BTMS studies in addition to studies evaluating the economic and environmental effects of utilising nanofluids in liquid BTMSs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The increased awareness of climate change concerns, coupled with the rapidly depleting and increasingly costly fossil fuels, in addition to the critical deficiencies related to conventional vehicles, led many countries to search for more sustainable transportation solutions [1–5]. Electric vehicles (EVs) serve as a promising contender, replacing conventional internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles [6–9]. Despite the numerous advantages of EVs, such as high energy efficiency and reduced carbon emissions [10,11], several issues hinder their widespread commercialisation; these issues include their relatively high cost compared to ICE vehicles, battery lifetime, travel distance and charging speed limitations [12–14], and most importantly safety concerns related to extreme weather and operating conditions [15]. Battery power either directly or indirectly influences all of these issues; therefore, extensive research and development needs to be conducted concerning the enhancement of EV batteries.

There are various options available for energy storage in EVs depending on the chemical composition of the battery, including nickel metal hydride batteries [16], lead acid [17], sodium-metal chloride batteries [18], and lithium-ion batteries [19]. Fig. 1 illustrates available battery options for EVs in terms of specific energy, specific power, and lifecycle, in addition to different battery shapes utilised in the EV industry. As is evident from Fig. 1, compared to other types, lithium-ion batteries possess exceptional properties and characteristics such as higher specific energy and power, and longer life cycle, in addition to some other valuable properties such as higher nominal cell voltage and shorter charging time [20–22]. Owing to these advantages, they are the commercially preferred option for EV energy storage [23].

Despite the numerous advantages, lithium-ion batteries suffer from a few temperature-related problems, namely, the high lifetime and capacity dependence on temperature [24,25], as well as safety and reliability issues related to extreme temperature operation causing harmful gas emissions and a phenomenon known as thermal runaway (the accelerated, unstoppable self-heating process of a battery after reaching a certain temperature limit (beyond 150 – 180 °C) [26,27]. The optimum temperature range for a safe and reliable operation falls roughly between 20 – 40 °C [28]. This relatively narrow temperature range highlights the importance of a robust thermal management system capable of maintaining the battery temperature within the optimum range irrespective of the operating (charging/discharging rate, propulsion/braking) and weather (hot/cold) conditions. Therefore, the Battery Thermal Management System (BTMS) has become a focal point of research and is of significant interest to electric vehicle manufacturers. Determining the maximum temperature and temperature difference of the battery is crucial for evaluating the performance of EVs [29,30].

To circumvent the temperature-related problems in order to achieve wider commercialisation, various BTMSs were proposed, developed, and optimised to meet the thermal demands of an EV battery. These systems include air [31–36], liquid [37–41], phase change material (PCM) [42,43], heat pipe (HP) [44,45], and thermoelectric [46,47] based systems each with pros and cons in addition to hybrid systems combining some of these types to avoid individual limitations and benefit from combined advantages [48–53]. Table 1 illustrates the main types of BTMS in addition to the advantages and disadvantages of each type [54,55].

In recent years, ample attention has been paid to the optimisation of liquid BTMSs in several ways, including structural optimisations [58], flow control optimisations [59], and more recently, the adoption of nanofluids as working fluids, owing to their higher heat transfer capabilities compared to conventional fluids, making them suitable for various engineering thermal applications [60–62]. Similarly, several thermal management studies considered the optimisation of heat sinks for electronic devices investigating different design parameters' effects on the system's cooling performance [63]. Many review papers in the field focused on summarising the latest research conducted on different types of BTMSs. Table 2 summarises some of the recent review papers in the field of EV BTMSs). A few were specialised in a specific type, such as liquid or PCM BTMSs [64–67], and even fewer were concerned with exploring the recent combined optimisation methods of liquid BTMSs utilising nanofluids, structural, and flow control methods [68,69]. Furthermore, a comprehensive review of thermal performance studies of nanofluid-based liquid BTMSs has been inadequately addressed.

This review paper aims to provide an up-to-date collection of advancements in utilising nanofluids/nanoparticles for the liquid cooling of Lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicle applications. Emphasis is placed on examining the thermal and electrical aspects of these batteries. The research investigates the multi-physical cooling performance of the BTMS, analysing factors such as maximum temperature rise and temperature difference, specifically focusing on the influence of nanoparticles. Additionally, the paper introduces BTMSs for electric vehicles that incorporate liquid cooling systems in conjunction with nanofluids.

This review starts with an examination of the latest studies in lithium-ion batteries with an emphasis on heat generation mechanisms and temperature-related deficiencies in different operating scenarios (Section 2). This discussion is followed by a section introducing different liquid BTMS analysis methodologies highlighting recent trends in optimisation studies (Section 3); the next section explores the recent studies related to nanofluids regarding preparation methods, stability analysis, thermophysical properties and simulation studies; this section is concluded with an overview and a detailed description of recent optimisation attempts of liquid BTMSs using nanofluids (Section 4). This discussion is followed by a section highlighting the most prominent research gaps, directions and trends in the field of nanofluid EV BTMSs (Section 5). Finally, the review provides a conclusion summarising the main points discussed throughout the paper (Section 6). Fig. 2 below illustrates the review structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat generation mechanisms and temperature deficiencies in lithium-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: The performance, lifetime, and safety of lithium-ion batteries depend greatly on their operating temperature; therefore, a precise understanding of heat generation mechanisms under extreme temperatures must be obtained to ensure the proper operation of EVs. In the following sections, recent studies related to these concepts will be overviewed and summarised.

Heat generation in lithium-ion batteries is a critical component affecting the overall operation of EVs and received and is receiving continuous attention from researchers [73–77]. Recently, researchers started analysing the heat generation behaviour of batteries under extreme charging/discharging and ambient conditions at different battery components. Lithium-ion batteries consist of a cathode, electrolyte, and anode. Typically, the cathode comprises lithium compounds such as lithium iron phosphate, lithium manganese oxide, and lithium cobalt oxide, while the anode is commonly made of graphite. Li et al. [73] studied the heat generation mechanism and battery failure related to the over and under-charging of a li-ion pouch battery (36 Ah). Experimental methods, including the Raman test, scanning electron microscopy, and X-ray diffraction, were used to analyse the tested battery cell. The study showed that heat generation associated with over-discharging was attributed to the dissolution of copper found in the anode and its subsequent deposition in the cathode. Similarly, Liu et al. [74] studied the effect of ageing and overcharging on heat generation mechanisms; the study was conducted on a li-ion pouch cell (1.2 Ah). Several analysis methods (scanning electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction) were used to analyse the tested battery cell. The study provided a detailed description of heat generation and thermal runaway during overcharging; it concluded that the activation for thermal runaway in overcharged cells is caused by the decomposition of the solid electrolyte interphase instead of a local internal short circuit. The study also showed that thermal runaway does not take place when overcharged at a temperature below the cathode decomposition temperature.

Yang et al. [75] developed a coupled thermo-electrochemical model to study the heat generation behaviour of lithium-ion battery components under different operating temperatures. The study was conducted numerically using detailed equations describing the thermo-electrochemical behaviour of a cylindrical lithium-ion battery cell (2.7 Ah). The study concluded that higher heat is generated at the positive electrode compared to the negative electrode, and the heat generated is largely attributed to polarisation during discharging. Furthermore, Wu et al. [76] studied the heat generation behaviour of a cylindrical lithium-ion battery (2 Ah) under 108 operating conditions using neural networks with the goal of developing a reliable heat generation model. The study was conducted both experimentally and numerically. The study concluded that the newly developed model to predict heat generation yielded less than 5 % error across all 108 cases compared to experimental data, validating the potential of utilising the developed model. Similarly, Wu et al. [77] developed a thermo-electrochemical model of a cylindrical lithium-ion battery (2.23 Ah), considering negative and positive electrode capacity ratios as well as different discharge rates. The study was conducted experimentally and numerically. The study concluded that a higher negative-to-positive capacity ratio reduces battery heat generation and that reversible heat generation plays a major role in overall heat generation and should not be neglected.

Based on the brief overview of recent studies in lithium-ion batteries heat generation mechanisms, it is evident that heat generation in cells can be attributed to three main components, namely, polarization, ohmic, and reversible heat generation components, with polarization generating the highest heat compared to other components. Fig. 3 summarises the heat generation components percentage for two studies [75,77].

It can also be observed from the literature that heat generation takes place on the positive and negative electrodes, electrolytes, and separators with varying degrees of intensity and higher heat generation is reported on the positive electrode followed by the negative electrode. Table 3 summarises the recent heat generation studies in terms of studied battery, type of study, studied variables and study outcomes.

While charging and discharging, a substantial amount of heat is generated due to chemical reactions as well as the flow of high electric current; when the battery is operated at extremely high temperatures (beyond 150 – 180 °C), it has the potential to cause thermal runaway [26]. Thermal runaway is a serious issue that can lead to fire and explosions, jeopardising the commercialisation of EVs. Therefore, researchers have devoted great efforts to better understanding its mechanism and causes with the goal of its prevention.

Liu et al. [78] experimentally investigated the thermal runaway mechanism of a cylindrical lithium-ion battery (2.5 Ah) under different states of health and charge as well as ambient temperatures. The study concluded that thermal runaway occurs through four consecutive stages with increasing temperatures and a sharp increase at the beginning of the fourth stage. The study also revealed that conduction and radiation modes of heat transfer are the most prominent in thermal runaway evolution. The main influencing factors affecting thermal runaway, according to the study, are side reaction accumulation, remaining reactants, and the battery surface temperature. Furthermore, Wang et al. [79] experimentally and numerically investigated the effect of overcharging a pouch lithium-ion battery (7 Ah) on thermal runaway. They utilised a multiparameter coupled model to conduct the study. The developed model proposed a four-stage warning system, and the investigation concluded that the thermal runaway can be prevented during the first three warning levels with varying degrees of damage and at the fourth stage the thermal runaway is unavoidable. The developed model depends on the detection of voltage, pressure, and temperature of the battery to detect signs of thermal runaway.

Kim et al. [80] numerically investigated the thermal runaway process of a pouch lithium-ion battery (5 Ah), focusing on the effect of combustion. The study concluded that battery temperature rises gradually until it reaches 190 °C, after which it spikes up to 509 °C. Considering the combustion heat, there was a sudden increase in temperature at about 60 °C which is speculated to be caused by changes in internal reaction patterns. The analysis of thermal runaway revealed that at low temperatures, the decomposition of solid electrolyte interphase significantly contributed to generated heat (90 % heat generated); at higher temperatures (170 °C to 200 °C), anode-electrolyte reactions played a major role in temperature increase. At extremely high temperatures (200 °C and above), the highest heat generated was due to cathode-electrolyte reactions. Similarly, Jeon et al. [81] developed an equivalent circuit model to investigate the thermal runaway evolution of a cylindrical lithium-ion battery (31 Ah). The model included resistance and capacitance terms to account for generated heat amount and maximum temperature occurrence time respectively. The study concluded that the maximum temperature during thermal runaway is directly proportional to the discharge rate, and the duration of thermal runaway is inversely proportional to the charging speed. The study also highlighted the severity of thermal runaway under overcharging conditions. Zhang et al. [82] experimentally and numerically investigated the thermal runaway evolution of a prismatic lithium-ion battery (59 Ah). The analysis revealed that, at the cell level within a module, cells experience simultaneous and consecutive propagation, whereas modules experience simultaneous propagation. The study also concluded that gas evolution drastically increases the cell pressure reaching up to 6.3 kPa and therefore proper ventilation is crucial for safe operation to prevent explosion.

The recent investigations of thermal runaway evolution in lithium-ion batteries under different operating conditions characterised the events of thermal runaway in terms of temperature, voltage, and pressure. Table 4 below provides a summary of key findings and recommendations of main research studies for the thermal runway on various battery types including experimental and modelling approaches. The following sections discuss the high and low operating temperatures.

The performance, efficiency, and safety of a lithium-ion battery are greatly diminished at high operating temperatures, extensive research has been conducted in recent years to better understand the mechanisms and causes of this degradation in order to develop better and more robust battery models.

Zhang et al. [86] studied the effects of high temperature on the ageing and safety of a pouch lithium-ion battery (3.9 Ah). The study was conducted experimentally under different discharge rates and the chemical reactions of the experiments were studied. The study reported that thermal safety is greatly reduced at higher temperatures due to lithium plating on the negative electrode in addition to the unstable condition of the positive electrode. The R-H+ reduction to H2 at the anode disturbs the distance between electrodes and depletes the electrolyte. Higher temperatures were also reported to decrease the thermal stability at the solid electrolyte interphase due to the lower thermal stability of products resulting from the reaction between lithium and electrolyte. Another study conducted on pouch battery type was done by Lalinde et al. [87] where they experimentally investigated the overcharged and high-temperature operation of a pouch lithium-ion battery (3.6 Ah). The investigated SOCs and temperatures fell in the range of 0 to 200 % and 25 to 100 °C respectively. The study examined varying SOC and ambient temperature scenarios and defined different regions in which key events take place. Five regions of interest were defined, namely, the safe operation region, accelerated degradation region, gas formation region, and finally thermal runaway region. The study revealed that safe operating temperature is reduced during charging (from 60 to 45 °C). Additionally, the study concluded that, for SOC below 100 %, battery degradation only depends on temperature.

Liu et al. [88] conducted an ageing study of a cylindrical lithium-ion battery (2.5 Ah) operating at high temperatures after being cycled at low temperatures. The study showed that factors affecting battery ageing at high temperatures are charging current, content of lithium plating, temperature, and discharging current. The study also highlighted the worse ageing effect of high-temperature operation after low-temperature cycling compared to that of constant high-temperature operation. Similarly, Ouyang et al. [89] experimentally studied the effect of high-temperature operation on the cycle life of a cylindrical lithium-ion battery (1.2 Ah) under different discharge rates. The study was conducted at 26 °C and 70 °C to investigate the effect of high-temperature cycling. The study concluded that ambient temperature and discharge rate have significant effects on the performance of the battery. More specifically, the high-temperature operation causes degradation that can be reflected as changes in the battery temperature (increase), and cycling voltage (left shift) in addition to internal resistance (degradation), compared to normal operating conditions.

Based on the review of recent studies on the high-temperature operation of lithium-ion batteries, it can be observed that high-temperature operation has been linked by many researchers to reduced performance, lifetime and safety. This overall degradation has been attributed to various factors such as increased lithium plating at the electrodes as well as deterioration of electrical properties (capacity, voltage, and resistance). Additionally, several studies conducted on high-temperature operation investigated the onset of thermal runaway and vice versa as both conditions are inextricably tied to one another.

Similar to high-temperature operation, low-temperature conditions greatly reduce the efficiency of lithium-ion batteries. This reduction in performance hinders the wider commercial adoption of EVs in cold-climate countries. Many studies were conducted on the low-temperature behaviour of lithium-ion batteries to obtain a better understanding of the limitations and develop strategies to circumvent them.

Guo et al. [90] experimentally and numerically investigated the operation of a cylindrical lithium-ion battery (2.4 Ah) under low-temperature conditions. They developed a thermo-electrochemical model to predict the electrical (voltage/ current) behaviour of the battery at low temperatures. The study reported that at extremely low temperatures (−20 and −40 °C), the battery discharge rate cannot be higher than 0.5C. Otherwise, the battery voltage will sharply decrease, reaching the cut-off voltage. The study also proved the validity of the numerical model, particularly at low discharge rates, compared to higher error at higher discharge rates, owing to the inaccuracy of assuming uniform particle distribution at higher discharge rates. The average error for voltage and temperature is 50 mV and 1 °C respectively.

Wang et al. [91] investigated the expansion of a pouch lithium-ion battery (1 Ah) during overcharging at low temperatures. The study was conducted experimentally and numerically. The study concluded that volume expansion occurs at lower charging rates when the battery is charged at low temperatures (1.5C for 25 °C, 0.5C for 10 °C, and 0.2C for 0 °C). The study also found a strong correlation between volumetric expansion and lithium plating. This correlation is further confirmed using scanning electron microscopy and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy of the tested batteries. Furthermore, You et al. [92] experimentally and numerically studied the nonlinear ageing of a pouch lithium-ion battery (1 Ah) during low-temperature operation. The study reported that irreversible lithium plating is the main cause of lithium loss and therefore degradation of the battery. The study also suggests that the increase in lithium plating is caused by a closed loop in which the negative electrode porosity experiences a reduction causing more lithium plating which forms a lithium plating layer further reducing the surface porosity of the negative electrode. This closed-loop causes an exponential increase in lithium plating.

The recent studies in low-temperature operation of lithium-ion batteries suggest that the main cause for performance deterioration of batteries under low temperatures is the phenomenon of lithium plating. Many studies explored a broad set of scenarios involving different charging/discharging rates and temperatures. Fig. 4 summarises various deficiencies related to lithium-ion battery operation under extreme temperatures (high/low), with an emphasis on the main events of thermal runaway evolution.

Fig. 4 summarises the effects of temperature on the performance and safety of a lithium battery over time. As shown in Fig. 4, the optimum operating temperature falls between 25 °C – 40 °C, below this range, at around −60 °C, the battery experiences negative effects such as reduced conductivity, structural irregularity, slowed reactions, dendrite growth, lithium plating, and electrolyte decomposition. These deficiencies lead to reduced battery performance and lifespan. On the other hand, operating temperatures exceeding 130 °C can lead to the formation of gas within the electrolyte and the meltdown of the separator, which exacerbates thermal instability in the battery. Further increasing the operating temperature i.e. 150 °C, the situation reaches a critical stage at which lithium reaches its melting point, at this point the temperature increase becomes a positive feedback loop where heat generation reactions become self-sustaining leading to fires and in extreme cases explosions [78,79].

This section explored recent developments in lithium-ion battery heat generation mechanisms in addition to thermal runaway evolution and extreme temperature operation deficiencies. Subsequently, the section highlighted the importance of maintaining the EV battery pack within the optimum temperature range under diverse operating conditions. This stark importance prompted researchers to optimise and improve existing BTMSs to accommodate extreme weather and operating conditions. The next section discusses recent experimental and numerical liquid BTMS optimisation studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Liquid BTMS analysis and optimisation methodologies
ce:section.ce:para: Liquid BTMSs received growing attention from the scientific community owing to the high thermal efficiency and excellent temperature uniformity making it the most commercially utilised mode of thermal management. There are two main types of liquid BTMS depending on the level of contact between the liquid and the battery namely, direct and indirect liquid BTMSs. Indirect liquid BTMS can be further classified into cold plate BTMSs and heat-conducting block with liquid channels BTMSs (with numerous flow direction arrangements including series, parallel, unidirectional, and bidirectional) [95–97]. On the other hand, direct liquid BTMS can be classified into immersion BTMS and mist/ spray BTMS [98,99]. Another method of classifying liquid BTMSs is based on the working fluid of the system. According to this classification, there are several types of BTMSs, most commonly water-based, ethylene glycol water-based, oil-based, and more recently nanofluid-based BTMSs.

Researchers have invested tremendous efforts to improve and optimise the different types of liquid BTMSs to meet the ever-growing demands of extreme operating conditions (fast charging/discharging and hot/ cold climate conditions). This section provides a general overview of experimental and numerical methodologies to investigate liquid BTMSs with a summary of recent studies adopting each methodology.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental methods used in liquid BTMS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental analysis involves the fabrication of a complete BTMS module containing the utilised components in real-life applications. A typical experimental setup consists of a battery module with cell numbers depending on the scale of the experiment, the selected liquid thermal management system for analysis (this includes all parts necessary to run the system such as a pump, a fluid storage unit, valves and connections as well as the actual system structure), an environmental chamber to create environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, wind speed) similar to actual weather conditions, a charging/discharging system to allow for battery module charging and discharging with real drive cycles to ensure accurate driving conditions (accelerating and decelerating). The setup also includes various measurement devices to obtain data for analysis and interpretation. These sensors include thermocouples (temperature measurement), multimeters (voltage and current measurements), flow meters (flow rate measurement), and anemometers (wind speed measurement). The sensors measurements are then collected using a data collection system. Fig. 5 illustrates a typical experimental setup for the purpose of studying a liquid BTMS.

Compared to numerical methods and despite the more realistic results obtained from the experimental analysis, the high fabrication cost in addition to the impractical design modification for performance optimisation reduces the number of BTMS experimental studies, additionally, the high energy consumption of the BTMS setup both to maintain the battery initial temperature and to operate the liquid pump for experimental analysis remains a challenge for large scale battery pack BTMS investigations. Table 5 below summarises some of the recent liquid BTMS studies conducted experimentally.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical methods used in liquid BTMS
ce:section.ce:para: Many researchers adopted the numerical approach to analyse liquid BTMSs due to the ease of setup and the expansive study possibilities. There are many numerical approaches used in research, the general framework of all the different methods includes the selection of analysis method and variables, setup of the model taking into account relevant phenomena, validating the model using experimental results, and finally investigating the various study parameters using the validated numerical model. There are two main types of numerical analysis commonly utilised in the literature: computational fluid dynamics (CFD) analysis studies using commercial software packages [97,102,103], and studies involving numerical solutions to analytical equations describing the studied system [104].

Ample research has been carried out using numerical analysis due to the relatively cheaper setup requirements as well as the rapid numerical solution convergence owing to the exponential advances made in the computational industry in recent years. Another recently emerging numerical method for analysis is the use of machine learning and algorithms to more rapidly investigate and examine liquid BTMSs taking into account a larger number of design variables [105]. The main challenges facing numerical BTMS studies include the lack of accurate model validation using experimental results in addition to the limitation of BTMS design parameters considered for analysis, parameters such as battery ageing and cycling influence on the BTMS performance. Table 6 summarises the recent numerical studies conducted in the field of liquid battery thermal management.

Optimisation methods of liquid BTMS can be categorised based on the different components of the system. This broad categorisation divides optimisation methods into structural, flow control, and working fluid optimisation. In the next subsections, the first two types will be investigated and a special case of the third method (working fluid optimisation using nanofluids) will be discussed in greater detail in the next main section.

Structural optimisation techniques received great attention from researchers. This method can be further classified into module formation variation, liquid channel design variation, the addition of fins to improve heat transfer potential, and immersion depth/spray height variation. The following are recent studies adopting different methods of structural optimisation.

Several studies were conducted on cylindrical battery modules. Angani et al. [100] experimentally investigated the effect of varying the BTMS structure of a 54 cylindrical lithium-ion battery module. The studied formations were zig-zag plates liquid BTMS, and bottom plate liquid BTMS (2 formations). They concluded that the hybrid cooling mode achieved the best performance and maintained the temperature uniformity below 1.4 °C, and maximum temperature at 34 °C. Cha et al. [101] experimentally and numerically investigated the effects of varying a 27 cylindrical lithium-ion battery BTMS module, 4 configurations of plate liquid BTMS were tested. The optimum liquid BTMS configuration maintained the maximum cell temperature at 55.2 °C, and improved efficiency by 28 %. Furthermore, Qin et al. [99] experimentally investigated the effect of varying the spray height of a 16 dummy cylindrical lithium-ion battery module spray liquid BTMS. They concluded that the optimum spray height depends on the amount of heat generated (for Qgen = 6 W Hoptimum = 5 cm – for Qgen = 12 W Hoptimum = 6 cm)

Likewise, some researchers considered pouch and prismatic battery modules. Wang et al. [98] experimentally studied the impact of immersion depth variation on a 5 pouch lithium-ion battery module immersion liquid BTMS. The study concluded that the optimum design conditions were achieved at 13.2 cm immersion depth with a maximum temperature and temperature uniformity of 39.4 °C and 1.23 °C respectively. Furthermore, Wu et al. [102] numerically investigated the performance improvement achieved by varying a prismatic lithium-ion battery cold plate liquid BTMS design compared to the base design, the optimised BTMS design reduced the temperature non-uniformity by 25.4 % and slightly reduced the highest temperature. Fig. 6. a illustrates the optimised maximum temperature under different discharge rates (1C, 2C, and 3C).

As can be seen from the preceding discussion, liquid BTMS structural optimisation studies focused on varying module design in terms of liquid channel shape and dimensions, additionally, direct contact systems received enhancement consideration in terms of immersion depth and spray height. Fig. 6 below summarises the optimisation results of some of the discussed studies in terms of the maximum temperature and temperature difference, flow velocity and direction in addition to module structure.

Fig. 6.a shows that the optimised BTMS design reduced the maximum temperature by an average of 0.33 % for the three discharge rates with improved performance under higher discharge rates. Fig. 6.b demonstrates that the parallel BTMS flow configuration achieved better overall performance with the 0.2 m/s velocity achieving the best performance for 32 battery cells maintaining the maximum temperature at 24.67 °C. Fig. 6.c shows that the maximum module temperature is inversely proportional to the velocity and that case 4 BTMS design maintained the lowest maximum temperature compared to the other designs, at 2 m/s inlet velocity, and case 4 design maintained the maximum temperature at 33 °C. Fig. 6.d illustrates that the optimised BTMS design lowered the maximum temperature by 5.7 K compared to the original design.

Flow control optimisation techniques can be divided into flow rate optimisation, direction variation optimisation, and velocity optimisation. Some studies also attempted to optimise the system performance by intermittently varying the flow direction. The following are recent flow control optimisation studies.

Fan et al. [96] experimentally investigated an 8 cylindrical lithium-ion battery cell single liquid cooling channel module. They explored the effect of varying flow rates on battery module temperature as well as the effect of the ratio between operating and stopping time for the BTMS (intermittent operation). They concluded that applying cooling with flow rates 500, 1000, and 2000 ml/min maintained the maximum temperature at 41.38, 41.57, and 40.88 °C respectively. They also determined that the best operation stopping time ratio was found to be 3 min/ 7 min, maintaining the maximum temperature at 42.86 °C and reducing pump operating time to 30 % thus improving efficiency. Moreover, Zeng et al. [110] numerically investigated the effect of varying flow direction, flow velocity, and operation duration on the performance of a 32 cylindrical lithium-ion battery liquid channels BTMS module. They investigated various configurations (unidirectional, bidirectional, and reciprocating flow). They concluded that by using the reciprocating flow, the power consumption and temperature non-uniformity were reduced by 15.6 % and 55.3 %. They also determined that the optimum design conditions were a 200 s period of reciprocation and 0.05 m/s flow velocity.

Similar to structural optimisation studies, flow optimisation studies also considered prismatic and pouch battery modules. Wang et al. [98] experimentally investigated a 5 pouch lithium-ion battery immersion liquid BTMS module. They investigated the effect of flow rate on system performance. They concluded that the optimum design flow rate was 0.8 L/min at which the maximum temperature and temperature uniformity were maintained at 39.4 °C and 1.23 °C respectively, Fig. 7 below illustrates the effect of spray height on BTMS thermal performance. Considering prismatic module studies, Guo et al. [105] numerically investigated a 6 prismatic lithium-ion battery module cold plate liquid BTMS They considered the effect of varying the flow velocity on the system performance. They concluded that the design case with 0.05 m/s maintained the lowest battery temperature (1.17 % lower than 0.03 m/s). Similarly, Li et al. [106] numerically studied an 8 prismatic lithium-ion battery module cold plate liquid BTMS. They considered the effect of flow direction variation on BTMS performance. They concluded that the optimum flow control condition maintained the maximum temperature at 308.79 K. Furthermore, the optimised BTMS design decreased the maximum temperature by 4.35 K and the maximum pressure drop by 1188.2 Pa.

Fig. 7.a shows that the maximum temperature is inversely proportional to immersion height and flow rate, furthermore, the 13.2 cm immersion depth and 1.2 L/min flow rate maintained the maximum temperature at 38.65 °C which is 32 % lower compared to the no cooling case. Fig. 7.b illustrates that the optimum spray height is 5 cm at which point the maximum temperature and temperature difference are 36.25 and 8.42 °C respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mitigation of thermal runaway using liquid BTMS
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed in Section 2.1, thermal runaway is a serious concern that hinders the wider commercialisation of EVs and as such it has become a main attention point for researchers. Only some studies specifically investigated the potential of preventing thermal runaway using liquid thermal management. The following are the recent studies on this topic.

Ping et al. [111] experimentally investigated the potential of mitigating thermal runaway of a 7 cylindrical lithium-ion battery module using a novel channel liquid BTMS. The BTMS acted as a conventional system in normal operation conditions (liquid flows in the channels and heat transfer takes place without contact between liquid and batteries). On the onset of thermal runaway, the film material surrounding the pipe melted and direct contact between the liquid and battery took place greatly improving the heat transfer and preventing the propagation of thermal runaway. The efficacy of the investigated system strongly depends on the melting temperature of the utilised film material, they studied four types of film materials. The study concluded that the optimum film material was polyethylene as it had a lower melting point than the thermal runaway temperature and thus prevented thermal runaway from affecting any of the module cells.

Liu et al. [112] experimentally studied the thermal runaway control potential of a cylindrical lithium-ion battery cell by using spray liquid BTMS. They studied the effect of atomizing air and water mist flow rates on the performance of the system. The study demonstrated the possibility of mitigating thermal runaway using 0.24 mL/s and 1.4 m/s mist and air flow rates respectively. These design conditions mitigated thermal runaway at the extreme temperature of 234.7 °C with a cooling rate of 0.53 °C per second. On the contrary, the system failed to prevent thermal runaway propagation when the temperature reached 250.8 °C, resulting in a maximum thermal runaway temperature of 471.5 °C.

Based on the preceding discussion, thermal runaway prevention using liquid BTMS received marginal consideration from researchers and as such presents a potential area of future research, especially taking into account additional factors such as battery SOC, ambient temperature and charging/discharging rate.

This section examined recent experimental and numerical study procedures of liquid BTMS highlighting the recent studies exploring different optimisation methods and their effects on system performance. Next, the potential of utilising nanofluids for improving the thermal performance of liquid BTMS will be discussed in detail regarding advances, limitations as well as future directions.

Nanoparticles play a major role in determining the performance of nanofluids [113,114], leading to an acceleration in research on their influence on nanofluid properties, primarily driven by advancements in nanotechnology and the increased availability of nanoparticles. The high surface area-to-volume ratio of nanoparticles, attributed to a significant number of atoms located at their boundaries, ensures their enhanced suspension stability, unlike their micrometre-sized counterparts [115]. The use of nanoparticles to enhance the thermal conductivity of conventional fluids was first accomplished by Choi et al. [116]. Since then, ample research has been conducted on nanofluids regarding various aspects including fabrication methods, stability enhancement, and thermophysical properties measurements as well as potential adoption in numerous engineering applications [117–120].

The issue of thermal management of EV batteries coupled with the ever-increasing charging and discharging currents demands challenged researchers for more effective and innovative solutions. One of these emerging solutions is the utilisation of nanofluids to improve the thermal performance of liquid BTMSs. The following sections will provide an up-to-date review of the recent studies in nanofluids concluded with a section discussing studies adopting nanofluids to determine the strengths and shortcomings of the technology in addition to delivering an outlook on prominent future research directions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation, thermophysical properties, and stability of nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: There are two fundamental methods of preparing nanofluids depending on the number of steps required, single-step approach and two-step approach. The single-step approach, as the name suggests, is performed by directly and simultaneously producing and dispersing nanoparticles within the base fluid. This method produces uniform stable nanofluids with low agglomeration at the expense of a lengthy production process and pressure range limitation [121] The two-step approach is the more commonly used way of producing nanofluids. In this approach, the nanoparticles are first produced and then separately dispersed in the base fluid. This method is preferred due to its simplicity at the expense of agglomeration and instability. [122].

The stability of nanofluids is a major challenge preventing their wider utilisation and as such received great research attention and effort to improve it under wide operating conditions. The addition of surfactants which prevent particle agglomeration is a possible remedy to the issue of nanofluid instability. There are several ways to evaluate the stability of a nanofluid ranging from simple visual inspection and centrifugation to more advanced methods such as dynamic light scattering and transmission electron microscopy. Fig. 8 illustrates the two commonly used nanofluid preparation methods.

Many studies investigated the one-step fabrication method, Zheng et al. [121] experimentally investigated the stability of water graphene nanofluids produced using the one-step approach (liquid shear exfoliation). They utilised CTAB surfactant. The study revealed that the stability of the produced nanofluids depends on the concentration of CTAB only up to 0.25 mg/mL, beyond this concentration, no noticeable improvement in stability is achieved (graphene concentration constant at 18 ppm). Furthermore, the study concluded that high-low temperature cycling could improve graphene nanofluid stability. Similarly, Seifikar [123] experimentally synthesised several polyethylene glycol-based nanofluids using the one-step fabrication method (laser ablation in liquids). They studied the potential of converting light to heat using different types of nanofluids. They concluded that nanofluids with silver nanoparticles showed the highest conversion potential with the nanofluid temperature reaching 52 °C after a 60-minute radiation period.

The two-step approach was also investigated by several studies. Shah et al. [124] experimentally investigated the properties of CuO water-based nanofluids. The studied weight concentrations were in the range of 0.01 – 0.05 %. The utilised stabiliser was gum Arabic using ultrasonic mixing. The stability of the nanofluids was maintained for six months. The study concluded that stability is inversely proportional to the nanoparticle's concentration. Furthermore, Ali et al. [125] experimentally studied the properties of ten graphene nanofluids focusing on rheological behaviour. The studied nanofluids were in the volumetric concentration range of 0.025 – 2.5 %. The study revealed that the viscosity of the nanofluid is affected by the regularity of the graphene sheets, higher defects in the sheets resulted in higher viscosity and vice versa.

As discussed in Section 4.1., nanofluids present a viable solution to the issue of thermal management of EV battery packs. Numerous studies both experimentally and numerically investigated the efficacy of utilising nanofluids in liquid BTMSs. This section provides a comprehensive overview of recent studies in the field.

Mitra et al. [126] experimentally and numerically investigated the utilisation of carbon-based ethylene glycol water nanofluids in a cylindrical lithium-ion battery module. The study investigated three volume concentrations (0.15 %, 0.3 %, and 0.45 %). The study concluded that the 0.45 % case showed the best thermal performance improvement with a temperature reduction of 11 °C (a 30.5 % temperature reduction compared to the base fluid with a 14 % increase in pressure drop) when utilising the dual channel with parallel flow configuration, Fig. 9 illustrates the experimental setup and temperature difference for the 0.45 % concentration case using different channel designs.

Furthermore, Liu et al. [127] experimentally investigated the performance enhancement achieved by utilising a water-based γ-Al2O3 nanofluid in a 7 prismatic battery liquid BTMS module. The study concluded that the optimum nanoparticles concentration was 2 % which improved the cooling efficiency by 8.16 %. Additionally, Kiani et al. [128] experimentally investigated battery module cooling using various water-based nanofluids under the influence of an alternating magnetic field. The study showed that compared to the water case, CuO and Fe3O4-based nanofluids improved the thermal performance by 12.6 % and 43.2 % respectively, with an increase of 55.2 % in pressure drop compared to the base fluid.

Yetik et al. [129] numerically investigated the potential of utilising engine oil-based Fe2O3-based nanofluid as the working fluid of a 15 prismatic battery liquid BTMS module. The study concluded that the optimum design conditions (0.04 m/s velocity, 5 % concentration, 2C discharge rate) maintained the maximum battery temperature at 294 K. Furthermore, Liao et al. [130] studied the performance enhancement achieved by using various water-based nanofluids in a 31 cylindrical battery liquid BTMS module. The optimum design conditions using Cu nanoparticles at 5 % concentration reduced the maximum temperature by 1.066 K and the temperature nonuniformity by 12.6 % compared to the base fluid performance. Tousi et al. [131] numerically investigated the utilisation of a water-based AgO nanofluid as a working fluid of a 12 cylindrical battery liquid BTMS module. The study concluded that the optimum design conditions at 0.28 m/s velocity maintained the maximum temperature and temperature nonuniformity at 305.59 K and 1.07 K respectively with a 31.8 % increase in pressure drop compared to the base fluid.

Sarchami et al. [132] numerically studied the optimisation potential of utilising water-based Al2O3 nanofluid in a 71 cylindrical battery liquid BTMS module. They concluded that using a nanoparticle concentration of 2 %, the maximum temperature and temperature nonuniformity were reduced by 1.2 °C and 0.4 °C respectively, while also increasing the pressure drop by 44.2 % compared to the base fluid. Rahmani et al. [133] numerically investigated the thermal improvement achieved by utilising water-based AgO nanofluid in a 10 cylindrical battery liquid BTMS module. The study determined that, at 3 % nanoparticles concentration, the nanofluid can improve the Nusselt number by up to 25 % compared to the base fluid performance. Moreover, Venkateswarlu et al. [134] numerically compared the performance of two ethylene glycol (EG) water-based nanofluids (Al2O3 and CuO). The study concluded that the CuO nanofluid achieved 8.2 % better thermal performance compared to that of the Al2O3 nanofluid.

Guo et al. [135] numerically investigated the performance of Al2O3 nanofluids as working fluids of a 6 prismatic battery liquid BTMS module. They examined a wide range of concentrations (1 – 5 %) and three base fluids (water, EG, and engine oil), additionally, they considered three different shaped nanoparticles (spherical, brick, and platelet). The study concluded that water-Al2O3 (5 % concentration) provided the best overall performance compared to EG and engine oil. Kumar et al. [136] numerically investigated the utilisation of hybrid nanofluids employing Al2O3, Cu, MWCNT, and graphene as dispersed nanoparticles with different concentrations in water as a base fluid. The adopted hybrid nanofluid was utilised in a 9 cylindrical battery liquid BTMS module. The study concluded that the optimum nanoparticles concentrations of Al2O3 (0.5 %), Cu (0.5 %) graphene (1 %) lowered the maximum temperature and temperature nonuniformity to 305.24 K and 5.23 K respectively, with a 70 % increase in pressure drop compared to the base fluid. Fig. 10 below illustrates the nanofluid performance results from the discussed studies.

The preceding discussion showed that recent nanofluid liquid BTMS studies primarily considered the nanoparticles type and concentration variation effect on thermal performance enhancement as well as on the increase of required pumping power. Furthermore, the discussion highlighted the lack of studies investigating hybrid nanofluid BTMSs. The following sections discuss in detail the outcomes of the previous studies in terms of the most commonly studied parameters such as concentration and type of nanoparticles and base fluid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of nanofluid concentration on system performance
ce:section.ce:para: The nanofluid concentration received the highest attention in recent nanofluid BTMS studies, Mitra et al. [126] three MWCNT nanofluid concentrations (0.15 %,0.30 %,0.45 %). The experimentally prepared nanofluids samples were found to be stable for more than 3 months with no sedimentation. The study concluded that increasing the concentration of nanofluid increases the fluid viscosity which results in a reduction in Reynolds number, it also resulted in an increase in heat transfer coefficient and thermal conductivity. The increase in heat transfer coefficient was attributed to the chaotic motion of nanoparticles accelerating energy and heat transfer. Similar concentration variation effects were reported by Liu et al. and Tousi et al. [127,131].

Kiani et al. [128] attributed the improvement in heat transfer with higher nanofluid concentrations to three factors, namely, nanoparticles Brownian motion, clustering of nanoparticles, and layers formation at the interface between nanoparticles and base fluid, all of these factors were found to improve with increasing nanofluid concentration. On the contrary, Yetik et al. [129] revealed that the nanofluid concentration, despite having a positive effect on system performance, had the least influence on system performance compared to factors such as inlet velocity and discharge rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of type of base fluid on system performance
ce:section.ce:para: The type of base fluid in which nanoparticles are dispersed to form the desired nanofluid can greatly affect the performance of the nanofluid. This fact led some researchers to investigate the effects of different base fluids on the nanofluid performance, these studies were, however, found to be considerably fewer compared to studies investigating the effect of nanofluid concentration on performance, highlighting a potential gap in the field. In this regard, Mitra et al. [126] studied the influence of utilising water compared to ethylene glycol (EG) water as a base fluid, they reported that the main limitation of water as a base fluid is the narrow operational temperature range (0 – 100 °C) compared to that of the EG water mixture (−16 – 104 °C), the study also revealed that the EG water mixture achieved better temperature uniformity as a base fluid compared to water, and this improvement was attributed to lower flow rate in the case of the mixture resulting from higher viscosity, in addition to the lower thermal conductivity of the EG water mixture, leading to as much as 6 % improvement in temperature uniformity. Furthermore, Guo et al. [135] investigated the performance of three different base fluids, namely, water, EG and engine oil, The study revealed that the water achieved the best performance compared to the other two base fluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of type of nanoparticles on system performance
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to the influence of the base fluid variation, the nanoparticles can also have a significant effect on the system performance. Kiani et al. [128] reported that nanoparticles with higher thermal conductivity (CuO) achieved better performance compared to lower thermal conductivity nanoparticles (Fe3O4), more specifically, the CuO nanoparticles maintained the battery temperature below 323 K up to 3080 s compared to only 2420 s for the case of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Similarly, Liao et al. [130] reported that copper-based nanofluids achieved the best performance compared to other metallic nanoparticles based nanofluids, highlighting the importance of nanoparticles thermal conductivity. Furthermore, Guo et al. [135] investigated the influence of nanoparticles shape on the nanofluid performance. They studied three Al2O3 nanoparticles shapes, namely, brick, platelet, and sphere. The study reported that the brick-shaped nanoparticles provided the best performance compared to the two other shapes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanofluids numerical simulation and validation methods
ce:section.ce:para: Examining the previously discussed numerical nanofluid BTMS studies, the modelling of nanofluids can be divided into two main methods, the more common single-phase approach, which involves the treatment of nanoparticles and base fluid as a single homogeneous continuum with effective thermophysical properties [129,130], the second approach is the mixture-based approach in which the nanofluid is treated as two separate phases with governing equations being solved for each phase individually [131], this is a more accurate modelling approach compared to the single-phase approach, however, it requires significantly more computing power as the governing equations are solved for each phase separately.

The validation of the nanofluid modelling approach using experimental results is critical in ensuring accurate simulation outcomes, this validation is usually done through comparing parameters such as the heat transfer coefficient and Nusselt number. Despite the importance of model validation, only a few nanofluid BTMS studies compared nanofluid simulation results to similar experimental results for validation. Fig. 11 below illustrates validation results from previously discussed nanofluid BTMS studies.

Section 4.2 and its subsections discussed nanofluid BTMS studies in terms of various aspects including studied nanofluid concentration, type of base fluid, type and shape of nanoparticles, as well as nanofluid modelling and validation methods. Table 7 below summarises the discussed studies in terms of battery modules, studied nanofluid types and concentrations as well as the study outcomes.

Based on the preceding review of nanofluid liquid BTMSs the following research gaps and directions are proposed:
•
Battery heat generation mechanisms and extreme temperature deficiencies studies need to consider large-scale battery modules to better represent actual EV battery packs, additionally, battery investigations must consider various battery conditions (SOC, SOH, and rate of discharge) to take into account diverse driving conditions and scenarios.
•
Nanofluid liquid BTMS studies need to take into account the effect of time on the system performance since the stability of nanofluids is greatly affected by long-duration operation which in turn can severely affect the BTMS performance. Furthermore, the nanofluid BTMS studies must consider both the heat transfer enhancement and the pressure drop increase for a more accurate BTMS performance evaluation.
•
Additionally, nanofluid liquid BTMS studies need to take into account the environmental and economic implications of using nanofluids as opposed to using conventional fluids as well as the influence of hybrid nanofluids on the BTMS performance, these considerations must take into account the preparation impact and increased pumping cost against the thermal performance enhancement.

This paper comprehensively reviewed the latest studies exploring nanofluid liquid BTMSs. The review started with a survey of recent analysis of heat generation mechanisms, thermal runaway evolution, and extreme temperature deficiencies in lithium-ion batteries highlighting the importance of thermal management which is then followed by recent liquid BTMS optimisation studies. The review concluded by inspecting the latest innovative liquid BTMSs adopting nanofluids. The following are the main review conclusions:
•
Heat generation in lithium-ion batteries can be attributed to three main components, namely, polarization, ohmic, and reversible with polarization generating the highest heat compared to other components. The highest heat generated is reported to take place in the positive current collector followed by the negative current collector. Furthermore, thermal runaway evolution can be divided into phases characterised by distinct cell properties (temperature, pressure, and voltage).
•
High-temperature operation of lithium-ion batteries has been reported to cause lithium plating on the negative electrode as well as instability on the positive electrode and solid electrolyte interphase. Similarly, low-temperature operation has been reported to cause a sharp voltage drop at high discharge rates, in addition to volume expansion and lithium plating.
•
Liquid BTMS structural optimisation studies focused on varying module design in terms of liquid channel shape and dimensions, additionally, direct contact systems received enhancement consideration in terms of immersion depth and spray height. Furthermore, flow control optimisation studies considered varying liquid flow rate, velocity, direction and operation intermittency.
•
Recent nanofluid-related studies focused on the preparation method which can be classified into one and two-step methods, stability and thermophysical properties (density, thermal conductivity, viscosity, and specific heat) of studied nanofluids. Furthermore, recent nanofluid liquid BTMS studies primarily considered the nanoparticles type and concentration variation effect on thermal performance enhancement with few studies considering the resulting increase of pumping power.
•
The recent studies investigating the utilisation of nanofluids in BTMSs demonstrated improved thermal performance with thermal enhancement in the range of 2.9 – 30.5 % compared to the base fluid. However, this improved thermal performance comes at the expense of higher pressure drop and energy consumption, specifically, the nanofluid pressure drop increased in the range of 14 – 70 % compared to the base fluid.
